question: An interrupting boundary event is attached to an embedded subprocess. While two parallel branches inside the subprocess are still active, the boundary event is triggered. What happens?

options.text: The entire subprocess, including every active element in both branches, is terminated and the flow continues from the boundary event.
Only the branch with the token nearest the boundary event is terminated; the other branch continues running.
The boundary event waits until both branches finish before completing the subprocess.
A new instance of the subprocess is created with the active elements rolled back to their initial state.

a.text: When an interrupting boundary event on an embedded subprocess is triggered, the entire subprocess — including all active elements inside it — is terminated and the outgoing flow of the boundary event is taken.

b.text: Interrupting boundary events do not terminate only a subset of branches. The entire enclosing subprocess and everything currently active inside it is terminated.

c.text: An interrupting boundary event does not wait for inner branches to finish; it terminates the subprocess immediately.

d.text: There is no automatic re-instantiation or rollback to an initial state; the subprocess simply terminates and the outgoing flow of the boundary event is taken.

explanation: Embedded subprocesses are commonly paired with boundary events. When an interrupting boundary event on the subprocess is triggered, the entire subprocess — every active element inside, in any branch — is terminated, and execution continues along the boundary event's outgoing flow.
